title: SCORE — Modeler / Senior Modeler Assessment
description: Purely HR/behavioral instrument, 30 questions at ~90 seconds each. Technical/software skill is assessed separately (technical test + Personal Interview), so this test carries no technical items at all. Weighted for an individual-contributor role: Team Play, Communication, and Problem-Solving get the most questions; Leadership is present at a lighter weight to spot early potential; Dispute Resolution, Organizational Mindset, and Ethics carry small dedicated quotas but ethics-concern flags can surface on any question, not just the dedicated ones.

sections.instructions: Choose the option that best reflects how you would actually act. There is no single 'correct' answer — pick the one truest to your instinct.

questions.prompt: A new teammate joins mid-project, unfamiliar with your studio's model standards, while you're on a tight deadline yourself. What do you do?

options.text: Point them to the standards documentation and tell them to work through it independently, since you can't spare the time right now.
Spend fifteen to twenty minutes walking them through the key standards now, then check back in once they've had time to apply them.
Ask a colleague who has more bandwidth today to handle the onboarding instead, so the new teammate still gets proper guidance.
Assume they'll ask if they run into trouble, and focus your attention on your own deadline for now.

questions.prompt: A colleague on another discipline's team is overloaded and asks for informal help outside your assigned tasks. What do you do?

options.text: Decline the request politely, explaining that it falls outside your assigned tasks and you need to stay focused on your own workload.
Jump in and help as much as they need, quietly absorbing the extra hours yourself without flagging the risk to your own deadline.
Offer a specific, time-boxed amount of help and let your own lead know about the trade-off so priorities stay visible.
Say you'll help 'later' to sound supportive, without actually committing to a concrete time or scope for that help.

questions.prompt: In a team meeting, credit for a successful clash-resolution effort gets attributed mostly to someone who did less of the actual work. What do you do?

options.text: Let it go and say nothing in the moment, deciding the credit mix-up isn't worth the awkwardness of raising it.
Correct the record right there in the meeting, emphasizing your own role firmly so people know what you actually did.
Calmly mention the fuller picture afterward, factually crediting everyone who contributed to the fix, including your own part.
Say nothing in the meeting, but vent about it privately to other teammates afterward instead of addressing it directly.

questions.prompt: You notice a teammate quietly struggling, and their slipping work is starting to threaten the team's shared deadline. What do you do?

options.text: Stay focused on your own tasks, reasoning that their workload and any slippage is really their own responsibility to manage.
Flag their struggling to your manager right away, without first talking it through with the teammate themselves.
Check in with them privately and directly, ask what's going on, and offer specific, practical support to help them catch up.
Quietly absorb their unfinished work yourself, without mentioning it to them, your manager, or anyone else on the team.

questions.prompt: Your project wins client praise, but a teammate who contributed less publicly claims most of the credit in front of others. What's your reaction?

options.text: Push back on their claim right there in front of everyone, correcting them sharply in the moment.
Let it go entirely and never bring it up with anyone, even though the credit-grabbing genuinely bothers you.
Calmly and factually acknowledge the whole team's contribution afterward, including your own part, without turning it into a confrontation.
Note the injustice quietly and start claiming extra credit yourself in future projects, telling yourself it just evens things out.

questions.prompt: Helping another discipline hit their deadline would require you to slightly adjust your own schedule, at some real cost to you. What's your approach?

options.text: Refuse the request outright, reasoning that their deadline pressure isn't really your problem to solve or accommodate.
Agree right away without asking any questions, and don't mention the real cost this shift will have on your own schedule.
Discuss the trade-off honestly with your lead and the other discipline, then agree on a compromise that actually works for both.
Agree to help, but reluctantly, and let your irritation about the cost show throughout the rest of the interaction.

questions.prompt: You disagree with a decision your team made collectively in a meeting you weren't part of. What do you do?

options.text: Disagree quietly with the decision and just implement it your own preferred way instead, without telling anyone.
Go along with it outwardly in front of the team, but vent your real objections to others behind the scenes.
Raise your concern constructively with the team or decision-maker afterward, then fully support the decision once it's been discussed.
Go along with the decision quietly, never voicing your view even briefly in the meeting when it was still open.

questions.prompt: You need to explain a complex clash issue to non-technical stakeholders (e.g. the client's facilities team) who don't understand BIM jargon. What's your approach?

options.text: Stick with the full technical terminology in your explanation, since you feel that's the most precise way to describe it.
Simplify heavily using plain analogies, avoid jargon entirely, and check they've actually understood before you move on to anything else.
Ask a colleague who's better at translating technical detail to handle the explanation for you instead.
Give a quick technical summary and move on to the next topic, regardless of whether they actually followed along.

questions.prompt: An important coordination decision was made verbally in a meeting. What do you do to make sure nothing gets lost?

options.text: Assume everyone will remember the verbal decision accurately, and skip writing anything down or circulating a summary afterward.
Write a brief but clear summary of what was decided and share it with everyone involved right after the meeting ends.
Write detailed notes for your own records only, keeping them private rather than sharing them with the rest of the team.
Wait for someone else on the team to take responsibility for writing up the meeting minutes instead of doing it yourself.

questions.prompt: A teammate's message about a coordination issue is unclear and could mean two very different things. What do you do?

options.text: Guess at what they most likely meant based on context, and proceed with the work without checking your assumption first.
Send a quick clarifying question right away and wait for their answer before you proceed with any of the work.
Proceed with whichever interpretation is easiest for your own schedule, regardless of what they most likely actually intended.
Leave the message unanswered for now and wait for them to follow up if it's actually important.

questions.prompt: Partway through a task, you realize the brief you were given was ambiguous and you've made an assumption that might be wrong. What do you do?

options.text: Keep pushing forward on your current approach and simply hope that your original assumption turns out to be correct.
Reach out early to confirm your assumption is correct, before you invest much more time building on top of it.
Finish the task using your own assumption, and only mention the ambiguity later if someone happens to ask about it.
Redo the task several different ways to cover every possible interpretation, without checking in with anyone about which is right.

questions.prompt: A client questions why a deliverable took longer than the timesheet estimate implied. What's the best response?

options.text: Tell them it was due to unspecified 'technical issues,' without going into any further detail about what actually happened.
Give a clear, honest breakdown of what the extra time actually involved and why, framed constructively rather than defensively.
Explain that the delay was mostly caused by a teammate's part of the work, without owning your own share of it.
Avoid answering the question directly and steer the conversation toward a different, less uncomfortable topic instead.

questions.prompt: Your manager's instructions for a task conflict with what the client previously told you directly. What do you do?

options.text: Follow your manager's instructions as given, without mentioning that they conflict with what the client told you earlier.
Follow the client's version instead, since they said it directly to you, without flagging the conflict back to your manager.
Point out the discrepancy to your manager as soon as you notice it, so it gets clarified before you proceed.
Pick whichever version seems technically easier to execute right now, without raising the conflict with either party.

questions.prompt: Two disciplines' models disagree on a critical dimension by 200mm, discovered the day before an issue-for-construction deadline. What's your first move?

options.text: Pick whichever number intuitively looks more correct to you, and proceed with issuing the set on that basis.
Escalate immediately to your lead with both models and screenshots, flagging it as blocking until the two disciplines jointly resolve it.
Issue the set as scheduled anyway, adding a note flagging the discrepancy for someone to look at afterward.
Hold off saying anything and wait to see whether someone else on the team notices the discrepancy first.

questions.prompt: You realize you misjudged the time a modeling task would take and are now behind, with the deadline in two days. What do you do?

options.text: Say nothing about being behind and just work faster, skipping some of your usual QA checks to try to catch up.
Inform your lead early with a realistic revised estimate, and ask together about reprioritizing scope or bringing in extra support.
Keep working quietly and wait until the actual deadline arrives to explain to anyone what happened and why.
When explaining the delay, attribute it mainly to an unclear brief at the start rather than your own time estimate.

questions.prompt: A software malfunction during a critical work session right before a deadline costs you an hour of unsaved work. What do you do?

options.text: Panic in the moment and immediately announce to the whole team that the deadline is now impossible to hit.
Quickly assess what's actually lost, try any alternate recovery options available, and give your lead a realistic revised estimate right away.
Quietly try to redo the lost work on your own, without adjusting the estimate or telling anyone what happened.
Blame the software malfunction openly to the team and just wait around for someone else to give you instructions.

questions.prompt: You're given conflicting information from two sources about a critical requirement (e.g. ceiling height) for the same space. What's your approach?

options.text: Pick whichever source happens to be more convenient for the work you've already done, without checking further.
Cross-check both sources against the master documents or BEP, and escalate to your lead if it's still unresolved after that.
Use whichever number was given to you most recently, regardless of how reliable that particular source actually is.
Split the difference and proceed with an average of the two numbers, without actually verifying either one.

questions.prompt: A task turns out to be more complex than expected, and you're not sure of the best technical approach. What do you do?

options.text: Take your best guess at an approach and just hope it works out well enough once you get further in.
Research standard approaches first, and if you're still unsure, ask a more experienced colleague before committing significant time to it.
Pick the first approach that comes to mind and keep going with it, even as new information suggests otherwise.
Quietly avoid picking up the task and hope that someone else on the team ends up handling it instead.

questions.prompt: You're assigned a task with insufficient information to complete it accurately, and the person who could clarify is unavailable for a day. The deadline is tomorrow. What do you do?

options.text: Make your best guess at the missing information and proceed with the task, without documenting it or telling anyone.
Make a reasonable, clearly-documented assumption, work cautiously on the lower-risk parts first, and flag the open question once the person is back.
Stop all work on the task entirely and wait until the person who can clarify it is available again.
Ask a random colleague who also doesn't actually know the answer, and treat their guess as if it were confirmed fact.

questions.prompt: A newer teammate keeps making the same mistake, creating rework for you. Even though you're not their manager, what do you do?

options.text: Quietly redo their work yourself each time it happens, since that feels easier than having an awkward conversation about it.
Report the recurring mistake directly to management, asking that the teammate be formally reprimanded for the repeated rework it causes.
Sit down with them, walk through the correct method together, and check their next few submissions before escalating to anyone else.
Point out the mistake openly during the coordination meeting so the rest of the team is aware it keeps happening.

questions.prompt: During a chaotic coordination crunch, no one has clearly taken charge of triaging what to fix first. What do you do, even without formal authority?

options.text: Hold back from stepping in and wait for someone more senior to take charge of the triage instead.
Step in to suggest a simple priority order for the group to align around right away, without waiting to be asked.
Focus only on getting your own piece sorted and leave it to the rest of the group to figure out priorities.
Take charge of the whole triage yourself and start assigning priorities without checking in with anyone else first.

questions.prompt: You disagree with a modeling standard your studio uses and believe your own method is better. What do you do?

options.text: Quietly switch to using your own preferred method instead, since you're confident it works better than the current standard.
Raise it with your lead or BIM manager, with reasoning and evidence, while continuing to follow the current standard during the discussion.
Complain to your colleagues informally that the standard feels outdated, without ever raising it formally with anyone who could change it.
Follow the existing standard as instructed, without ever bringing up your concern with your lead or BIM manager.

questions.prompt: A junior colleague asks you, an experienced modeler, whether they're ready to take on more responsibility. What do you do?

options.text: Give a quick, generic 'sure, you're ready' answer and move the conversation on to something else fairly fast.
Take the time to give specific, honest feedback based on what you've actually observed in their work, and suggest one concrete next step.
Redirect them to ask their manager instead, since that feels like a more appropriate conversation for you to have.
Give a softened, overly positive answer rather than an honest one, mainly to avoid hurting their feelings right now.

questions.prompt: In a coordination meeting, a structural engineer insists a beam position is fixed and MEP must reroute around it — even though that reroute would violate minimum ceiling clearance required by code. What do you do?

options.text: Agree in the meeting to avoid conflict and go along with the reroute, despite knowing it creates a code clearance issue.
Push back firmly, insisting structural has to move the beam, without offering the group any alternative options to consider.
Clearly flag the code clearance conflict with technical justification, and propose two or three alternatives for the group to evaluate together.
Stay quiet about the code conflict during the meeting itself, and raise the concern privately with your manager afterward instead.

questions.prompt: During a late-night coordination call under deadline pressure, a colleague is visibly stressed and starts snapping at others. What do you do?

options.text: Ignore the tension entirely and stay focused on your own part of the call, deciding it isn't your problem to manage.
Snap back sharply in the moment to defend the person being criticized, matching the stressed colleague's tone.
Acknowledge the stress calmly, redirect the conversation back to the specific issue, and suggest a short break if things stay tense.
Say nothing to the colleague during the call, but report their behavior to management right afterward instead.

questions.prompt: Two teammates on your project openly disagree about the right modeling approach in front of you and ask you to weigh in. What do you do?

options.text: Stay carefully neutral and avoid picking a side, even though you privately think one approach is clearly more correct.
Give your honest technical opinion based on the actual merits of each approach, while staying respectful of both people's perspectives.
Side with whichever teammate happens to be more senior, regardless of which approach actually has the stronger technical merits.
Escalate the disagreement straight to your lead, without first offering your own technical opinion to either teammate.

questions.prompt: A recurring coordination issue keeps causing delays across multiple projects, not just yours. What's the best individual response as a modeler (not yet a manager)?

options.text: Keep manually solving the issue each time it resurfaces on your own projects, without looking at the wider pattern.
Document the recurring pattern and its impact across projects, and proactively suggest to your lead that it's worth fixing at the source.
Assume it's someone more senior's job to notice the pattern across projects, and say nothing about it yourself.
Complain about the recurring issue informally to teammates, without ever raising it as a formal process concern to your lead.

questions.prompt: Your studio is under resourcing pressure across several projects at once, and you finish your own task early. What do you do?

options.text: Consider your own part of the work done, and simply wait for your next task to be assigned to you.
Proactively check with your lead whether another project or teammate could use your help, given the studio-wide resourcing crunch right now.
Use the unexpected spare time on personal tasks of your own, without mentioning to anyone that you finished early.
Sit tight and wait to be told what to do next, rather than asking your lead if help is needed elsewhere.

questions.prompt: At 6pm you discover an error in the shared central model that will affect three other disciplines' work if not fixed tonight. Fixing it means at least 2 unplanned overtime hours. What do you do?

options.text: Log off at your normal time and plan to mention the error in tomorrow's coordination meeting, since no one asked you to stay late.
Fix it tonight yourself, but just leave the extra hours off your timesheet this once so it doesn't look like you're padding it.
Notify your coordinator right away about the issue and its downstream impact, offer to fix it tonight, and log your actual hours honestly.
Go ahead and fix it tonight on your own, but don't bring it up with anyone unless somebody happens to ask.

questions.prompt: Your manager asks you to sign off on a coordination model as 'clash-free,' but you know a few unresolved clashes remain in a low-visibility area. What do you do?

options.text: Sign off as 'clash-free' since your manager asked you to, reasoning that it's technically their call to make, not yours.
Decline to sign off as requested, and clearly document the remaining clashes and their risk before anyone makes a final decision.
Sign off as asked to keep things moving, but quietly mention the unresolved clashes to a colleague just so someone else knows.
Hold off responding to your manager for now, hoping the sign-off question somehow resolves itself or someone else handles it.
